title: Please write essay that explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to establish a…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Please write essay that explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to establish and maintain a competitive advantage in the competition with Apple. In so doing, illustrate the inter-dependencies via an action-reaction sequence of events. 
Your essay should critically 1. assess the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools. 
2. outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts introduced in this Module. 
3. illustrate how the transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can be used to reshape strategy. 
The best way to approach the assignment is to focus on concerns may have to do with (i) market entry (ii) product launch (iii) challenges of technology or (iv) prices. So, 1. proceed to analyse the market-asa-game, 2. identify and filter the competitive threat, 3. assess the action-reaction sequence of events and 4. evaluate your results. And always 5. provide a risk assessment of the likely competitive threats facing a company on choice of strategy.

Please must make citation from the following articles:

Akshay R., R. (2000). How to Fight a Price War. Harvard Business Review,.
Bill, G. (2014). How to Outsmart Activist Investors. Harvard Business Review,.
Dan, A. (2009). The End of Rational Economics. Harvard Business Review, 87(7).
Feng, Z. (2019). Why some platforms thrive… and others don’t . Harvard business review,.
Hanna, H. (2014). THE LIMITS OF SCALE. Harvard Business Review,.
Kevin P., C. (2009). Predicting Your Competitor’s Reaction. Harvard Business Review,.
McNutt , P. (n.d.). Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions, Second Edition.
McNutt, P. (n.d.). Economics of Strategy.
Tang, C.S. (2010). A review of marketing–operations interface models: From co-existence to coordination and collaboration. International Journal of Production Economics, 125(1), pp.22–40. doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpe.2010.01.014.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Title: Samsung and Apple: Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory in the Battle for Market Supremacy

Introduction

The rivalry between Samsung and Apple has gained widespread attention due to their continuous battle in the smartphone industry. Both companies have employed various price and non-price strategies to establish and maintain a competitive advantage. This essay aims to explore how Samsung executes these strategies in response to Apple, using the lens of transaction cost economics and game theory concepts. In doing so, the interdependencies and action-reaction sequences of events will be illustrated, while critically assessing the value-added of these economic tools and highlighting the overlaps between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts. Lastly, the implications of these theories for reshaping strategy will be discussed.

Market as a Game and Action-Reaction Sequence

In the market-asa-game perspective, companies like Samsung and Apple interact and react to each other's decisions, with each seeking to maximize their profits and market dominance (McNutt, Economics of Strategy). One action-reaction sequence can be observed in the pricing strategies of both companies. According to Akshay R. (2000), price wars mainly occur among market leaders because each company aims to undercut their competitors in order to gain market share. In the case of Samsung and Apple, Samsung has set its prices lower than Apple's iPhones, thereby attracting more price-sensitive consumers (Rao, 2000). In reaction, Apple introduced lower-tier iPhones, such as the iPhone SE, to counter this threat.

Competition on the technology front is another key contention between Samsung and Apple. Samsung has continuously sought to challenge Apple by launching a series of products featuring new technology, such as foldable devices. Apple, in response, has also stepped up its innovation game, releasing a series of iPhones with improved computational capabilities, such as the iPhone XS and XR (Feng, 2019).

Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory

Transaction cost economics is a tool that seeks to understand the optimal design of organizational structures and their impact on reducing transaction costs (Williamson, 1979). In this context, it is crucial for Samsung and Apple to assess their own transaction costs when developing new products and implementing pricing strategies (Hanna, 2014). Analyzing transaction costs can help both companies reduce costs and improve their overall competitiveness.

Game theory is a powerful tool for understanding strategic interactions between competitive agents (McNutt, Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions). In the case of Samsung and Apple, game theory concepts can help predict their competitor's reactions, informing their own strategic decisions (Coyne, 2009). For instance, Apple may predict that Samsung would react to the launch of a lower-priced iPhone by further lowering its prices, leading to a price war. Consequently, Apple may opt for alternative strategies such as targeting niche markets or differentiating its products.

Overlap Between Micro-Economics and Game Theoretic Concepts

Micro-economics deals with the analysis of individual decision-making, while game theory focuses on strategic interactions between multiple decision-makers (Ariely, 2009). Although these two disciplines may seem distinct, they share overlapping concepts that are applicable in the Samsung-Apple rivalry. For instance, the notion of Nash equilibrium, a central concept in game theory, is derived from micro-economics, as it describes a situation in which no player has an incentive to change their strategy given the strategies of others (McNutt, Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions).

Another area of overlap between micro-economics and game theory can be seen in the concept of price discrimination, a micro-economic concept that can be employed in game theory models as well. Samsung and Apple can apply this concept to better understand their strategic pricing decisions, allowing them to better predict competitor reactions and resultant market outcomes (Tang, 2010).

Reshaping Strategy with Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory

Transaction cost economics and game theory can be used to enhance a company's strategy. By analyzing transaction costs and considering game theoretic predictions when making strategic decisions, Samsung and Apple can better anticipate their competitor's moves, while simultaneously optimizing their own actions.

In line with price concerns, companies must consider multiple scenarios by analyzing competitor reactions and examining transaction costs in order to strategically select pricing and product launch decisions. Companies should utilize both transaction cost economics and game theory tools in combination with other economic frameworks, such as demand elasticity, to enhance their predictive capabilities, and ultimately reshape their overall strategy.

Conclusion

The Samsung-Apple rivalry presents an excellent example of the application of both transaction cost economics and game theory concepts. By leveraging these tools, both companies can make improved strategic decisions, ultimately captivating the ever-evolving market. The action-reaction sequence of events highlights the critical interdependencies between the two companies and illustrates the necessity of understanding one's competitor to achieve strategic success. As technological advancements continue to shape the global landscape, both companies must remain vigilant in predicting and reacting to the challenges and opportunities this competitive rivalry presents.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Title: Samsung and Apple: Competitive Strategies in the Battle for Market Supremacy

Introduction

The rivalry between Samsung and Apple has garnered widespread attention due to their continuous battle for dominance in the smartphone industry. Both companies have employed various price and non-price strategies to establish and maintain a competitive advantage. This essay aims to explore how Samsung executes these strategies and assesses the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools within this context. Additionally, the essay will outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts and illustrate how these frameworks can be used to reshape strategy. Focusing on market entry, product launch, challenges of technology, and prices, the analysis will proceed with the market-asa-game, identification and filtration of competitive threats, action-reaction sequence of events, and evaluation of results, while providing a risk assessment of likely competitive threats facing the company on choice of strategy.

1. Market-asa-Game

The market-asa-game perspective views competition between companies, such as Samsung and Apple, as a series of strategic interactions wherein each seeks to maximize their profits and market dominance (McNutt, Economics of Strategy). In such a framework, firms must anticipate and react to the strategies deployed by their competitors, thus leading to a complex web of interdependencies. The smartphone market, with its intense competition between Samsung and Apple, serves as a prime example of a market-asa-game (McNutt, Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions).

2. Identifying and Filtering Competitive Threats

To achieve and maintain a competitive advantage, Samsung must identify and filter the competitive threats posed by Apple and other smartphone manufacturers. Price competition, innovation, market segmentation, and market positioning are some of the key aspects Samsung must consider in this analysis (Akshay R., 2000). By analyzing consumer preferences and pinpointing unique selling points of competitors, Samsung can devise strategies to differentiate themselves and address the competitive threats more effectively (Hanna, 2014).

3. Action-Reaction Sequence of Events

The action-reaction sequence of events can be illustrated through the pricing strategies employed by Samsung and Apple. Samsung has traditionally aimed to set its prices lower than Apple's iPhones to cater to more price-sensitive consumers (Rao, 2000). In reaction, Apple has introduced lower-cost iPhones, such as the iPhone SE, to counter this threat. In turn, Samsung's launch of innovative technologies, such as foldable devices, prompts Apple to continually invest in product development to keep pace (Feng, 2019). This series of action and reaction further emphasizes the interdependencies between these two market leaders (Coyne, 2009).

4. Value-Added of Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory Tools

Transaction cost economics (TCE) provides a framework for analyzing the design of organizational structures in reducing transaction costs (Williamson, 1979). In the context of Samsung's competition with Apple, understanding and managing transaction costs is fundamental to developing new products and implementing pricing strategies efficiently (Hanna, 2014). TCE can help both Samsung and Apple minimize costs and enhance their overall competitiveness.

Game theory is a powerful tool for understanding strategic interactions, allowing players to anticipate their competitor's reactions when making decisions (McNutt, Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions). By adopting game theory concepts, Samsung and Apple can better predict each other's strategic moves and inform their own decision-making processes (Coyne, 2009).

5. Overlap Between Micro-Economics and Game Theoretic Concepts

Micro-economics and game theory share overlapping concepts, such as the Nash equilibrium, which describes a situation in which no player has an incentive to change their strategy given the strategies employed by others (McNutt, Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions). Another area of overlap is price discrimination, as both frameworks rely on it to study pricing decisions and market outcomes. Samsung and Apple can utilize these overlapping concepts in conjunction with game theoretic models to better understand their own and their competitor's strategic decision-making process (Tang, 2010).

6. Reshaping Strategy Using Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory

Transaction cost economics and game theory can be coupled to inform and reshape Samsung's competitive strategies. By analyzing transaction costs associated with their operations and employing game theory predictions, Samsung can optimize its decision-making processes while anticipating Apple's strategic moves. When devising pricing strategies, product launches, technological investments, and market positioning initiatives, Samsung can benefit from exploring multiple scenarios, evaluating their impact on transaction costs, and considering implications from game theoretic perspectives. Such analyses can guide Samsung towards more informed, comprehensive, and robust strategic decisions (Dan, 2009; Bill, 2014).

7. Risk Assessment of Competitive Threats

In their continuous quest for market dominance, both Samsung and Apple face various risks associated with their choice of strategy. These risks include potential price wars, intellectual property disputes, shifting consumer preferences, regulatory challenges, and evolving market demands. To mitigate these risks, Samsung must closely monitor market trends, regulatory changes, and competitor activities, while continuously investing in product innovation, diversification, and cost-effective operations (Hanna, 2014).

Conclusion

The Samsung-Apple rivalry epitomizes the use of price and non-price strategies to establish and maintain market dominance. Using transaction cost economics and game theory tools, both companies can make improved strategic decisions, positioning themselves at the forefront of the evolving smartphone market. By leveraging concepts from micro-economics and game theory, coupled with a keen awareness of the action-reaction sequence of events, Samsung and Apple can navigate the complex interdependencies within this highly competitive industry. In conclusion, firms must remain vigilant, as the dynamic nature of the smartphone market necessitates continuous adaptation, innovation, and informed decision-making for long-term success.
